metadata.title: 40th Report of the Delegated Powers and Regulatory Reform Committee
metadata.author: HL

chunks.text: The Delegated Powers and Regulatory Reform Committee The Committee is a ppointed by the House of Lords each session and has the following terms of reference: (i) To report whether the provisions ofanybill inappropriately delegate legislative power, or whether they subject the exercise of legislative power to an inappropriate degree of parliamentary scrutiny; (i i) To report on documents and draft orders laid before Parliament under or by virtue of: (a) sections 14 and 18 of the Legislative and Regulatory Reform Act 2006, (b) section 7(2) or section 19 of the Localism Act 2011, or (c) section 5E(2) of the Fire and Rescue Services Act 2004; and to perform, in respect of such draft orders, and in respect of subordinate provisions orders made or proposed to be made under the Regulatory Reform Act 2001, the functions performed in respect of other instruments and draft instruments by the Joint Committee on Statutory Instruments; and (i i i) To report on documents and draft orders laid before Parliament under or by virtue of: (a) section 85 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998, (b) section 17 of the Local Government Act 1999, (c) section 9 of the Local Government Act 2000, (d) section 98 of the Local Government Act 2003, or (e) section 102 of the Local Transport Act 2008. Members Baroness Bakewell of Hardington Baroness Humphreys Lord Carlile of Berriew Lord Janvrin Lord Cunningham of Felling The Earl of Lindsay Lord Goodlad Lord McLoughlin (Chair) Lord Hendy Lord Rooker Registered Interests Committee Members’ registered interests may be examined in the online Register of Lords’ Interests at https://www. parliament. uk/hlregister . The Register may also be inspected in the Parliamentary Archives. Publications The Committee’s reports are published by Order of the House inhardcopy and on the internet at www. parliament. uk/hldprrcpublications . General In for mation General in for mation about the House of Lords and its Committees, including guidance to witnesses, details of current inquiries and forthcoming meetings is on the internet at http://www. parliament. uk/business/lords/ . Contacts for the Delegated Powers and Regulatory Reform Committee Any query about the Committee or its work should be directed to the Clerk of Delegated Legislation, Legislation Office, House of Lords, London, SW1A 0PW. The telephone number is 020 7219 3103. The Committee’s email address is hldelegatedpowers@parliament. uk . His to rical Note In February 1992, the Select Committee on the Committee work of the House, under the chairmanship of Earl Jellicoe, noted that “in recent years there has been considerable disquiet over the problem of wide and sometimes ill-defined order-making powers which give Ministers unlimited discretion” (Session 1991–92, HL Paper 35-I, paragraph 133). The Committee recommended the establishment of a delegated powers scrutiny committee which would, it suggested, “be well suited to the revising function of the House”. As a result, the Select Committee on the Scrutiny of Delegated Powers was a ppointed experimentally in the following session. It was established as a sessional committee from the beginning of Session 1994–95.

It was established as a sessional committee from the beginning of Session 1994–95.

chunks.text: powers scrutiny committee which would, it suggested, “be well suited to the revising function of the House”. As a result, the Select Committee on the Scrutiny of Delegated Powers was a ppointed experimentally in the following session. It was established as a sessional committee from the beginning of Session 1994–95. The Committee also has responsibility for scrutinising legislative reform orders under the Legislative and Regulatory Reform Act 2006 and certain instruments made under other Acts specified in the Committee’s terms of reference.

chunks.text: ONLINE SAFETY BILL: GOVERNMENT AMENDMENTS 1. We reported on this Bill in our 28th Report of the current Session. It establishes a new regulatory regime to address illegal and harmful content online. As introduced, the Bill provided for the regulation of the following kinds of internet service providers: social media services, search services and services which provide pornographic content. The Bill contains detailed provision relating to the regulation of these kinds of internet service providers. It imposes a variety of duties, including a duty to minimise user contact with illegal content and duties concerned with minimising content which is harmful to children. The duties which apply vary depending on the nature of the service provider. The Bill provides for OFCOM to be the regulator and it confers functions on OFCOM for this purpose, including a wide range of investigative and en for cement powers. 2. The Bill was a mended during Report stage to include a delegated power to allow for the regulation of app stores. The relevant provisions are contained in clauses 216 and 217 of the Bill as amended on Report. Although the relevant amendments were tabled on 5 July, we were not sent the supplementary delegated powers memor and um relating to them until 17 July, the day on which the amendments were debated in the House. It was therefore not possible to for us to report before they were debated. 3. The power to regulate app stores is linked to a duty imposed by clause 162 on OFCOM to report on theuseofapp stores by children. Regulations mayonlybemade if the Secretary of State, having considered that report, considers there is a material or significant riskofharm to children presented by harmful content on app stores or from apps available in app stores. 4. This is a significant Henry VIII power, since it allows the Secretary of State by regulations to amend any provision of the Billtomake provision for or in connection with the regulation of app stores. The Bill does not specify how app stores are to be regulated, save that clause 217(3) provides that only OFCOM can be the regulator in relation to app stores. Otherwise, the Bill leaves all the detail of the policy for the regulation of app stores tobesetout in the regulations, including which app stores are to be to subject to regulation, the duties to be imposed on app stores and the functions of OFCOM in regulating app stores. 5. In leaving all the detail tobesetout in regulations, clauses 216 and 217 constitute skeleton legislation. As noted in our report, “ Democracy Denied? ”, it is our view that, as a matter of principle, the principal aspects of policy should be on the faceofabill leaving only its detailed implementation to delegated legislation. Skeleton legislation, which (as in this case) leaves the policy almost entirely to regulations, should onlybeused where it is 28th Report , (Session 2022–23, HL Paper 160). Democracy Denied?

chunks.text: matter of principle, the principal aspects of policy should be on the faceofabill leaving only its detailed implementation to delegated legislation. Skeleton legislation, which (as in this case) leaves the policy almost entirely to regulations, should onlybeused where it is 28th Report , (Session 2022–23, HL Paper 160). Democracy Denied? The urgent need to rebalance power between Parliament and the Executive (12th Report, Session 2021–22, HL Paper 106). See para 65 of the report. DELEGATED POWERS AND REGULATORY REFORM COMMITTEE accompanied by a full justification for adopting that approach, including why no other approach was reasonable to adopt. 6. The Department’s reasons for including a delegated power to regulate app stores aresetout in the supplementary memor and um. They are based on the need to allow OFCOM to report before legislating to ensure that there is an adequate evidence base to inform the legislative provision that is made. However, given that the Government accept the principle that app stores may require regulation, we would have expected the memor and um to explain why this evidence gathering could not take place before the Bill’sintroductionso the detailed provisions relating to the regulation of app stores could have been included on the face of the Bill. We consider it to be a matter of regret that this was not the approach adopted, particularly as this was the approach adopted for the other internet services regulated by the Bill. We urge the Minister to take the opportunity during the remaining stages of the Bill to explain to the House why steps were not taken to determine the policy on the regulation of app stores before the Bill’s introduction. 7. We make clear in “ Democracy Denied ?” our view that skeleton legislation should be a voided. Accordingly, we consider it would be far preferable for the regulation of app stores to be dealt with in future primary legislation, rather than being left to subordinate legislation. We also wish to record our disappointment that we were prevented from reporting on these amendments prior to Report stage because of the Department’s failure to provide the supplementary delegated powers memor and um in a timely fashion. See para 67 of the report. See paras 9 to 12 of the section in the supplementary memor and um relating to the app stores amendment. DELEGATED POWERS AND REGULATORY REFORM COMMITTEE Appendix: MEMBERS’ INTERESTS Committee Members’ registered interests may be examined in the online Register of Lords’ Interests at https://www. parliament. uk/hlregister . The Register may also be inspected in the Parliamentary Archives. For the business taken at the meeting on 29 August 2023, Members declared no interests. Attendance The meeting was a ttended by Baroness Bakewell of Hardington Mandeville, Lord Carlile of Berriew, Lord Cunningham of Felling, Lord Goodlad, Lord Hendy, Baroness Humphreys, Lord Janvrin, The Earl of Lindsay, Lord McLoughlin and Lord Rooker.
